author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
title: The Fourteenth Breviate.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “Again the question must be asked,” he says, “If man’s nature is good, as nobody but Marcion or Manichæus will venture to deny, in what way is it good if it is impossible for it to be free from evil?

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: For that all sin is evil who can gainsay?”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: We answer, that man’s nature is both good, and is also able to be free from evil.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Therefore do we earnestly pray, “Deliver us from evil.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: This deliverance, indeed, is not fully wrought, so long as the soul is oppressed by the body, which is hastening to corruption.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: This process, however, is being effected by grace through faith, so that it may be said by and by, “O death, where is thy struggle?

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Where is thy sting, O death?

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The sting of death is sin, and the strength of sin is the law;” because the law by prohibiting sin only increases the desire for it, unless the Holy Ghost spreads abroad that love, which shall then be full and perfect, when we shall see face to face.
